:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 680 people who qualified for parole but remained incarcerated for a new court commitment. This group made up 39%% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

A prisoner serving a “time” sentence may not be paroled until they have served at least 1/4 of that sentence. In Montana, a sentencing judge who imposes a state prison term of more than one year may also impose the restriction that the offender is ineligible for parole and participation in the supervised release program while serving the term. Separate sentences must generally run consecutively. If an offender receives a consecutive sentence after incarceration but before a parole ruling is made on the initial sentence, parole eligibility is determined by aggregating the original sentence and the consecutive sentence. However, if the parole ruling on the initial sentence has already been made, the offender will not be eligible for parole on the consecutive sentence until after the offender discharges the original sentence unless a hearing panel orders otherwise. Due to the fact that an offender may still be eligible for parole on the first sentence, the hearing panel may allow the second term to commence for the purposes of calculating parole eligibility. Note, however, that this does not shorten the length of the consecutive sentence.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new court commitment, accounting for 74% (496 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Overview ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, there were 272 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 32% of the parole-eligible prison population.

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
